text: We would like to thank the Society for the honor of consideration of our work.
Today we are presenting a robot-assisted transabdominal preperitoneal or TAP ventral hernia repair for a patient with a multi-recurrent incisional hernia with incarcerated bladder.
We have no disclosures relevant to this presentation.
Our patient is a sixty-two-year-old male with history of an open appendectomy in nineteen eighty-two, complicated by a multi-recurrent incisional hernia.

He presented to our hernia center with a complaint of a one-year history of a right lower quadrant bulge associated with only mild pain at baseline, but with increasing hesitancy and pain associated with voiding.
Past medical history includes type two diabetes, GERD, BPH, and hyperlipidemia.
He is a never smoker with a BMI of thirty-one point three.
Surgical history included the aforementioned open appendectomy, as well as a primary umbilical hernia repair.
Previous incisional hernia repairs included three separate laparoscopic repairs, all with intraperitoneal mesh placement.
Upon evaluation in clinic, the patient was noted to have a partially reducible right lower quadrant incisional hernia recurrent at the site of previous open appendectomy.
Preoperative CT confirmed the recurrent hernia with evidence of incarcerated bladder and also noted significant intraperitoneal mesh.
The patient was taken to the operating room with plan to complete a robotic tap right-sided TAR and mesh excision.
After induction of anesthesia, the surgeon completes bilateral TAP blocks and places a Foley catheter.
The surgeon gains abdominal access using a Veress needle at the left costal margin, followed by insufflation of the abdomen to a pressure of fifteen millimeters of mercury.
An 8mm robotic port is inserted in place of the varus needle, and the surgeon inspects the abdominal cavity using a 30-degree robotic laparoscope.
Under direct visualization, the surgeon places additional eight millimeter ports in the left mid abdomen and left lower quadrant.
The da Vinci robotic system is docked on the patient's left flank, preparing for the robotic-assisted surgical procedure.

The surgeon uses a combination of sharp, blunt, and electrocautery dissection to take down adhesions between the small bowel and the abdominal wall, many of which are attached to previously placed mesh.
The surgeon initiates the right-sided retrorectus dissection to create the first of two planned myofascial flaps, using robotic assistance.
The surgeon uses cautery to divide the fused linea alba adjacent to the hernia, revealing the underlying rectus muscle.

The surgeon releases the posterior rectus sheath from the overlying rectus muscle using scissors with cautery, extending the dissection plane to a level five centimeters above the furthest extent of the defect.
The surgeon continues lateral dissection until the neurovascular bundles are exposed at the lateral extent of the rectus muscle, completing the first myofascial flap.
The surgeon performs a right-sided transversus abdominis release (TAR), focusing on the dissection and release of the transversus abdominis muscle.

The surgeon uses electrocautery and sharp dissection to divide the transversus abdominis muscle medial to the neurovascular bundles in the upper abdomen, revealing the muscle's separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon extends the dissection superiorly to the level of the xiphoid process and inferiorly to the arcuate line, continuing the division of the fused linea alba adjacent to the hernia.
The dissection exposes the transversalis fascia and the underlying peritoneum, revealing these anatomical layers in the surgical field.
The surgeon mobilizes the transversus abdominis muscle anteriorly while creating a wide peritoneal flap laterally, extending into the retroperitoneum.
An assistant port is placed in the right mid-abdomen under direct visualization, followed by the introduction of a Raytech surgical sponge into the abdominal cavity.
A surgical instrument is positioned in the retromuscular plane to provide traction, aiding in the development of the transversus abdominis release (TAR).
The surgeon focuses on reducing the hernia sac and its contents, including the incarcerated bladder, within the surgical field.

The surgeon performs meticulous dissection and adhesiolysis using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, along with electrosurgical energy, carefully avoiding injury to the contents of the hernia sac.
The surgeon carefully excises the previous mesh and tacks, freeing them from dense adhesions to the bladder and preperitoneal adipose tissue following the reduction of the hernia sac.
The surgeon extends the dissection inferiorly beyond the hernia defect, exposing the pubic symphysis and the myopectineal orifice, and laterally to reveal the psoas muscle.
The surgeon completes the second myofascial flap, facilitating generous mesh overlap and placement within a virgin plane.
The surgeon excises additional components of the previously placed mesh, carefully freeing them from attachments to the bladder that was previously incarcerated within the hernia sac.
The surgeon cuts the excised mesh in a pinwheel fashion to facilitate its removal through the assistant port, ensuring the mesh can be extracted efficiently.
The surgeon encloses the anterior rectus sheath, including all visible defects, using a running suture technique with a number one Stratafix.
The surgeon incorporates the hernia sac into the closure to eliminate dead space and reduce the risk of seroma formation.
The surgeon desufflates the abdomen to an insufflation pressure of ten millimeters of mercury, reducing the abdominal cavity's inflation for the next surgical step.
Methylene blue is infused into the previously incarcerated bladder, visibly staining the bladder tissue.
The surgeon inspects the fully distended abdomen for signs of injury, looking for any extravasation of methylene blue as an indicator.
The surgeon cuts the progrit mesh to the appropriate size and inserts it into the abdomen through the assistant trocar located in the right upper quadrant.
The mesh is unfolded and positioned behind the defect, ensuring excellent overlap with the surrounding tissue.
The abdomen remains desufflated to ten millimeters mercury insufflation pressure, maintaining a reduced intra-abdominal pressure during this phase of the procedure.
The surgeon carefully unrolls the mesh in the desired location within the abdomen, ensuring it lies flat and closely approximates the overlying tissue without redundancy, bulging, or laxity.
The surgeon introduces a suture for closure of the posterior sheath through the assistant port. The port is then removed, and the site is covered by the inserted mesh.
After placing the mesh, the surgeon ensures hemostasis within the surgical field, checking for any bleeding around the mesh placement site.
The surgeon re-approximates the posterior sheath to the anterior abdominal wall using a running III-O stratific suture, ensuring the layers are securely joined.
The surgeon removes the remaining eight-millimeter ports under direct visualization, leaving the last camera port in place.
The surgeon fully desufflates the abdomen, indicating the completion of the procedure and the removal of insufflation gases.
The surgeon closes the skin of all port sites using a running subcuticular suture with monocryl.
The patient tolerated the procedure well and was discharged post-op day one.
At four-week follow-up, he expressed no current concern for hernia recurrence, was voiding normally, and steadily returning to previous activity consistent with an expected post-operative course.
